narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's model predicted a 75% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, which is below the typical 3.21 average for students in the dataset, and your LSAT score of 20, far lower than the usual 36.70. Your school decile in Year 1 was 5, a bit under the average of 5.71, but your Year 3 decile of 1 stands out as especially low compared to the typical 5.53, placing you in the bottom tier among peers. Coming from a middle-income family, being Asian, female, and planning full-time studies aligns somewhat with common profiles—females make up about 44% and Asians around 6%, while full-time is the norm at 93%—but didn't shift the outcome much. The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure was your Year 3 decile, as being in the lowest group strongly signaled challenges ahead. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, which also nudged the prediction toward failure since it was below average. Your low LSAT score ranked third, reinforcing concerns about your preparation for the rigors of law school and the bar. Family income and racial background followed closely, each contributing toward the failure prediction in your case, while your undergraduate GPA was sixth but still added to the risk. Interestingly, your gender as female was the only factor that slightly pulled in a positive direction, toward a higher chance of passing. Overall, these elements combined to make your profile riskier than most students', who tend to have stronger mid-to-high deciles, higher test scores, and GPAs. To improve your chances next time, focus on realistic steps like boosting your academic performance to climb higher deciles, retaking the LSAT for a better score, and strengthening your GPA through additional coursework or post-bac programs. With targeted effort in these areas, you could realign your profile closer to successful applicants and reapply stronger. Keep pushing forward—you have the determination to turn this around.
